title: Cat Biting Paws Until Raw: Causes, Urgency, and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your cat biting their paws until they are raw? Discover common causes, urgency levels, and why professional triage is essential for your pet.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat bites their paws until the skin is raw, it is a clinical sign of intense discomfort known as over-grooming or self-mutilation. This behavior is typically triggered by extreme itchiness (pruritus), physical pain, or psychological distress. Common underlying causes include environmental or food allergies, parasitic infestations such as fleas or mites, fungal infections like ringworm, or even behavioral issues like feline hyperesthesia or anxiety.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While it is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency, your cat is in significant discomfort and at high risk for secondary bacterial infections. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice active bleeding, foul-smelling discharge, or if your cat is limping and lethargic, you should seek care more promptly.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-resolution photo of the affected paws can be a vital tool for triage. Visual evidence allows a veterinary professional to assess the severity of tissue damage, identify patterns of hair loss, and look for signs of infection such as swelling or pus. These details help determine the necessary speed of intervention and can assist the vet in preparing the appropriate diagnostic tests before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I use human antiseptic on my cat's raw paws?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using human medications or ointments without veterinary approval, as many contain ingredients that are toxic to cats if licked and ingested.

faqs.question: How can I stop my cat from biting their paws right now?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent further self-trauma until your vet appointment is to use an Elizabethan collar (cone) to physically block them from reaching their paws.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to this behavior?
faqs.answer: While any cat can develop these symptoms, some breeds may be more predisposed to underlying conditions like allergies or stress-related behavioral disorders.
